strInstructions: Heat oil in pan and sauté garlic and onions. Add chicken and sear on all sides until lightly browned. Pour in vinegar, soy sauce, and water. Add bay leaves, pepper, and Knorr Chicken Cubes. Bring to a boil, reduce heat, and simmer uncovered for 10 minutes. Remove chicken pieces from sauce and fry in another pan until nicely browned. Return the chicken to the sauce, add sugar, and simmer again for another 10 minutes or until the sauce thickens. Serve warm.

Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Brown the potatoes and carrots lightly, then remove and set aside. Cook the bell peppers briefly and set aside. In the same pan, sauté onion and garlic until softened. Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned. Add the tomatoes and cook until softened and juicy. Stir in fish sauce. Pour in water, bring to a boil, then lower heat and simmer until the chicken is tender and the sauce reduces slightly. Return the potatoes and carrots and cook until tender. Add the bell peppers and sweet peas and cook until heated through. Season with salt and pepper, then simmer until the sauce reaches your desired consistency.

Trim excess fat from the chicken and pat dry. Heat oil in a pot over medium heat, then sauté onion, garlic, and ginger until fragrant and softened. Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce and cook briefly. Add the rice and stir for a minute or two. Pour in the water and bring to a boil, skimming any foam that rises. Add the bouillon cubes and stir to dissolve. Lower the heat, cover, and simmer until the rice breaks down and the chicken is tender, stirring occasionally. Season with salt and pepper. Serve hot with hard-boiled egg, fried garlic bits, chopped green onions, and calamansi or lemon wedges.

Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Brown the potatoes and carrots, then remove and set aside. Sauté onion and garlic until softened. Add the beef and cook until lightly browned. Pour in the tomato sauce, tomato paste, and water. Bring to a boil, then lower heat and simmer until the beef is tender. Add the minced Thai chili peppers, olives, and liver spread, stirring until the sauce is smooth. Return the potatoes and carrots and continue cooking until tender. Add the cheese and stir until melted into the sauce. Add the bell peppers and cook until tender-crisp. Season with salt and pepper before serving.

In a pot, bring the chicken stock and soy sauce to a boil. Add the rice noodles and cook just until softened, then drain and reserve some of the soaking broth. In a wok or wide pan, heat oil and cook the shrimp until pink; remove and set aside. Cook the pork hamonado until lightly browned and set aside if needed. Add onion and garlic and cook until softened. Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned. Stir in the carrots, celery, and cabbage and cook briefly until tender-crisp. Return the shrimp and pork to the pan. Add the noodles and reserved broth a little at a time, tossing gently until the noodles are cooked through and the liquid is absorbed. Season with salt and pepper, garnish with green onions, and serve with calamansi or lemon wedges.

Bring the water to a boil in a deep pot over medium heat. Add the glutinous rice and stir so the grains are distributed evenly. Lower the heat and cook, stirring from time to time, until the rice begins to soften and the mixture thickens. Add the tablea chocolate and continue stirring until melted and fully blended into the porridge. Stir in the sugar and cook until dissolved and the rice is tender. Serve hot with evaporated milk drizzled on top.

Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Add onion, garlic, and ginger and cook until softened and aromatic. Add the chicken and cook, stirring occasionally, until the color changes and juices run clear. Add fish sauce and cook briefly. Pour in the water and bring to a boil, skimming any scum that rises to the top. Lower the heat, cover, and simmer until the chicken is tender. Add the green papaya and cook until just tender. Add the spinach leaves and cook until wilted. Serve hot.

In a large pot, combine pig face, vinegar, soy sauce, crushed garlic, peppercorns, bay leaves, salt, and enough water to cover the meat. Bring to a boil, removing any scum that rises. Lower heat, cover, and simmer until tender. Drain well and discard the liquid. Grill the pork until crisp and lightly charred, or broil in the oven until nicely browned. Let cool, then chop into small pieces. In a bowl, combine the chopped pork, onions, chili peppers, calamansi juice, and liquid seasoning. Add liver spread and mix gently until combined. Season with pepper to taste. Transfer to a sizzling plate and serve hot.

Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Add annatto seeds and cook until the oil is infused with color, then remove and discard the seeds. Add onions and garlic and cook until softened. Add the pork and cook until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce and cook briefly. Add the tomatoes and cook, mashing occasionally, until softened and juicy. Pour in the water, bring to a boil, then lower heat, cover, and simmer until the pork is tender. Add the potatoes and carrots and cook until almost tender. Add the liver, bell peppers, garbanzo beans, and raisins. Cook until the liver is done and the sauce thickens. Season with salt and pepper and serve hot.

In a bowl, combine the ground chicken, green onions, water chestnuts, carrot, garlic, soy sauce, salt, and pepper until evenly mixed. Separate the wrappers into individual sheets. Place about a heaping tablespoon of filling near the edge of a wrapper and roll tightly into a thin log, sealing the end with a little water. Repeat with the remaining filling and wrappers, then cut each roll into about 3-inch lengths. Heat about 2 inches of oil in a pan over medium heat. Fry the lumpia in batches, turning as needed, until golden brown and crisp and the filling is fully cooked. Drain on a wire rack and serve hot.

Sort through the dried gabi leaves and shred them into pieces, discarding any stray leaves. Prepare the lemongrass by removing the tough outer layers, then pound and chop the tender parts. In a wide pan over medium heat, warm the oil. Add onion, garlic, and ginger and cook until softened and aromatic. Add the pork belly and cook until lightly browned. Season with pepper, then stir in the shrimp paste and cook briefly. Add the coconut milk, dried gabi leaves, lemongrass, and Thai chili peppers. Do not stir at first; let the leaves absorb the liquid. Simmer until the leaves are tender and most of the liquid has reduced. Add the coconut cream and continue cooking until thickened and creamy. Season with salt to taste and serve hot.

Rinse the oxtail well and trim excess fat. In a pot, boil the oxtail in enough water, skimming off impurities as they rise. Lower the heat and simmer until the meat is fork-tender, adding water as needed. Cook the banana heart, eggplant, long beans, and pechay separately until just tender, then set aside. In a pan, toast the rice flour lightly until fragrant. In another pan, heat oil and sauté onion and garlic until softened. Add annatto powder diluted in a little broth, peanut butter, toasted rice flour, and some of the meat broth. Stir until smooth and thickened. Add fish sauce and season with salt and pepper. Combine the cooked oxtail with the peanut sauce and simmer briefly. Arrange the vegetables around the stew and serve hot with shrimp paste on the side.

Rinse the pork ribs well. In a pot, combine pork and enough water to cover, then bring to a boil and skim off the foam. Once the broth clears, add tomatoes, onion, and fish sauce. Lower the heat and simmer until the pork is tender, adding more water as needed. Add gabi and cook until almost tender. Add finger chilies and radish and continue simmering briefly. Add the long beans, then the eggplant and okra, cooking just until the vegetables are tender. Add the tamarind or tamarind base and stir until dissolved. Season with salt and pepper, then add pechay and cook until wilted. Serve hot.

Place the beef shanks in a large pot with enough cold water to cover. Bring to a boil, then drain and rinse the meat and pot to remove impurities. Return the beef to the pot with fresh water, onion, fish sauce, and peppercorns. Bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer until the beef is very tender and the marrow softens. Add the corn and continue to cook until tender. Season with salt to taste. Add pechay and green onions and cook just until wilted. Serve hot with fish sauce, calamansi juice, and chopped chili peppers on the side if desired.

Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Sauté onion and garlic until softened. Add the ground pork and cook, breaking it apart, until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce and cook briefly. Add the tomatoes and cook until softened and juicy. Pour in the water and bring to a simmer. Add the potatoes and carrots and cook until tender and the sauce slightly reduces. Season with salt and pepper. Serve hot.

Heat oil in a pot over medium heat. Sauté onion and garlic until softened, then add the tomatoes and cook until they break down. Add the shrimp and cook just until the color changes. Stir in fish sauce. Pour in water and bring to a simmer. Add the kalabasa and cook until starting to soften. Add the long beans, Thai eggplant, ampalaya, okra, and patola in stages as needed, cooking until the vegetables are tender but not mushy. Stir in the coconut milk and simmer gently until the sauce becomes creamy. Season with salt and pepper and serve hot.

In a pot over medium heat, combine the pork, onion, garlic, peppercorns, bay leaves, water, vinegar, and soy sauce. Bring to a boil without stirring too much. Lower the heat, cover, and simmer until the pork is tender and the liquid reduces. In a separate pan, heat oil and brown the pork pieces lightly if you want deeper color and flavor, then return them to the sauce. Pour in the coconut milk and add the chopped chili peppers. Simmer gently until the sauce thickens slightly and the flavors come together. Season with salt if needed and serve hot.

Heat oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Sauté onion and garlic until softened, then stir in fish sauce. Add the coconut milk and chili peppers and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook until slightly reduced. Add the kalabasa and cook until beginning to soften. Add the sitaw and continue cooking until both vegetables are tender. Add the shrimp and cook just until pink and fully done. Season with salt to taste and serve hot.

Cut a slit in the center of each beef cube and insert a strip of pork fat. Heat oil in a pot and brown the potatoes and carrots lightly, then remove and set aside. Sauté onion and garlic in the same pot until softened. Add the beef and cook until lightly browned. Add calamansi or lemon juice and soy sauce and cook briefly. Pour in tomato sauce, water, and bay leaves, then bring to a boil and skim any impurities. Lower the heat, cover, and simmer until the beef is tender. Return the potatoes and carrots and cook until tender. Add the bell peppers and cook until just tender-crisp. Season with salt and pepper and serve hot.

In a bowl, combine the pork with sugar, salt, garlic powder, pepper, and food coloring until the meat is evenly coated. Transfer to a covered container or resealable bag and refrigerate overnight to cure. To cook, place the pork and any marinade in a pan with enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer until the meat is tender and the liquid is mostly absorbed. Add oil and continue cooking, stirring regularly, until the pork caramelizes and browns lightly. Serve hot, ideally with garlic rice and egg.

Prepare the lemongrass by trimming the tough parts and finely chopping the tender portion. In a bowl, combine vinegar, calamansi juice, garlic, ginger, lemongrass, brown sugar, salt, and pepper. Add the chicken and marinate in the refrigerator for several hours. To make the annatto oil, render chicken skin or fat in a pan, then add annatto seeds, crushed garlic, and bay leaf. Let the oil steep, then strain. Grill the marinated chicken over hot coals, turning and basting with annatto oil until cooked through and lightly charred. Let the chicken rest briefly before serving with rice and extra chicken oil.

Marinate the chicken in lemon or calamansi juice and soy sauce. Brown the chicken in oil, then set aside. In the same pan, sauté onion and garlic until aromatic. Add the chicken back, then pour in water or broth and simmer until tender. Add hot dogs, carrots, potatoes, mushrooms, and bell peppers. Stir in table cream or evaporated milk and cook until the sauce becomes rich and creamy. Season with salt and pepper and serve hot with rice.

Marinate chicken pieces in pineapple juice, then drain and reserve the marinade. Heat oil in a pan and sauté onions and garlic until softened. Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned. Pour in the reserved pineapple juice and simmer until the chicken is tender. Add pineapple chunks, carrots, bell peppers, and cream or evaporated milk. Continue simmering until the sauce thickens slightly. Season with salt and pepper and serve with rice.

Heat oil in a pot and lightly brown the potatoes and carrots, then set aside. Sauté onion, garlic, and ginger until fragrant. Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce and curry powder. Pour in coconut milk and water, then simmer until the chicken is tender. Return the potatoes and carrots and cook until done. Add bell peppers and cook briefly. Adjust seasoning with salt and pepper and serve hot.

Heat oil in a wide pan and sauté onions, garlic, and ginger until aromatic. Add pork belly and cook until lightly browned. Stir in shrimp paste and cook for a few minutes. Pour in coconut milk and simmer until the pork is tender and the sauce begins to reduce. Add chili peppers and continue cooking until the sauce is thick and creamy. Pour in coconut cream and simmer until rich and oily. Season as needed and serve with steamed rice.

Season pork belly with salt and pepper. Sear the pork in a wide pan until lightly browned. Add pineapple juice, soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, onions, and peppercorns. Bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer until the pork is tender. Add pineapple chunks and continue cooking until the sauce reduces into a sweet and savory glaze. Slice or serve the pork pieces with the sauce.

Boil the pork belly until tender, then drain and reserve some broth if needed. In a pan, sauté onions, garlic, and tomatoes until softened. Add the pork and cook until lightly browned. Stir in shrimp paste and cook until fragrant. Add vinegar and let it boil uncovered. Add a little water or broth and simmer until the sauce thickens and coats the pork. Add chili peppers if desired and adjust seasoning before serving.

Place pork belly in a pot with water, garlic, bay leaves, peppercorns, and salt. Boil until the pork is tender, then drain well. Let the pork cool and dry completely. Heat oil in a deep pan and fry the pork belly until the skin becomes crisp and golden. Remove from oil and let it rest before chopping. Serve with lechon sauce or spiced vinegar.

Clean the pork leg and place it in a pot with water, garlic, onions, bay leaves, peppercorns, and salt. Boil until the meat is tender but still intact. Drain and dry the pork leg thoroughly. Rub with salt and let it air-dry or chill to help crisp the skin. Deep-fry carefully until the skin is golden, blistered, and crisp. Drain well and serve with soy-vinegar dipping sauce.

Sauté onions, garlic, and ginger in oil until aromatic. Add pork and cook until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce and cook briefly. Pour in vinegar and let it boil without stirring. Add water and simmer until the pork is tender. Stir in pork blood gradually while mixing to keep the sauce smooth. Add chili peppers and continue simmering until the sauce thickens. Season with salt and pepper and serve with puto or rice.

In a large bowl, combine ground pork, beaten eggs, bread crumbs, raisins, carrots, bell pepper, relish, cheese, salt, and pepper. Spread the meat mixture on foil and arrange hot dogs and boiled eggs in the center. Roll tightly into a log and seal the foil ends. Steam until fully cooked. Cool before slicing, or pan-fry the slices lightly before serving.

Place beef shanks or brisket in a pot with water and bring to a boil, skimming impurities. Add onion and peppercorns, then simmer until the beef is tender. Add corn and potatoes and cook until softened. Add cabbage, pechay, and green beans near the end so they remain tender-crisp. Season with fish sauce or salt and serve hot with steamed rice.

Season beef cubes with salt and pepper. Heat oil and butter in a pan, then cook garlic until fragrant. Add the beef in batches and sear quickly over high heat. Add Worcestershire sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning, then toss until the beef is coated and just cooked. Do not overcook so the beef stays tender. Serve immediately with garlic rice.

Boil beef with water until tender, reserving the broth. In another pan, sauté garlic, onion, and ginger. Add the tender beef, soy sauce, sugar, star anise, and some broth. Simmer until the sauce becomes slightly thick and glossy. Adjust seasoning with salt and pepper. Serve the beef with garlic fried rice and a bowl of hot beef broth.

Clean the fish, season with salt, and fry until golden and crisp. Set aside. In another pan, sauté garlic, onion, and ginger until fragrant. Add bell peppers and carrots, then cook briefly. Pour in vinegar, water, sugar, and ketchup if using. Simmer until the sauce is balanced sweet and sour. Add the fried fish and spoon the sauce over it, or pour the sauce over the fish before serving.

Clean the bangus and carefully remove the meat from the skin without tearing it. Marinate the fish skin in calamansi juice and soy sauce. Flake the fish meat and remove bones. Sauté onion, garlic, carrots, peas, raisins, and the flaked fish until cooked. Mix with eggs and seasonings. Stuff the mixture back into the fish skin and sew or secure the opening. Fry until golden and cooked through, then slice and serve.

Clean the squid and drain well. In a bowl, combine soy sauce, calamansi juice, garlic, sugar, salt, and pepper. Marinate the squid briefly. Stuff the squid with chopped tomatoes and onions, then secure with skewers or toothpicks. Grill over hot coals, basting with the marinade or oil, until the squid is cooked and lightly charred. Serve with spiced vinegar or calamansi-soy dip.

Season bangus and fry or grill until cooked. Flake the fish meat and discard bones. In a pan, sauté onions, garlic, and chili peppers until aromatic. Add the flaked bangus and cook until lightly crisp. Season with calamansi juice, soy sauce, mayonnaise, and pepper. Transfer to a sizzling plate if desired and top with more onions or chili.

Rinse mung beans and simmer in water until soft and beginning to break down. In another pan, sauté onion, garlic, and tomatoes until softened. Add pork or shrimp and cook until lightly browned. Stir in fish sauce. Add the cooked mung beans and broth, then simmer until flavors blend. Add ampalaya leaves, malunggay, or spinach and cook until wilted. Serve with fried fish or rice.

Heat oil in a wok and sauté onion and garlic until fragrant. Add chicken, pork, shrimp, or liver if using, and cook until done. Add the firmer vegetables first, such as carrots, cauliflower, and broccoli. Stir in cabbage, bell peppers, snow peas, and young corn. Pour in broth and season with oyster sauce, soy sauce, salt, and pepper. Add a cornstarch slurry and cook until the sauce thickens and coats the vegetables.

Heat oil in a pan and sauté onion, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes until softened. Add pork if using and cook until lightly browned. Stir in shrimp paste and cook until fragrant. Add squash and water, then simmer until the squash begins to soften. Add eggplant, ampalaya, okra, and long beans. Cover and cook until the vegetables are tender but not mushy. Adjust seasoning and serve hot.

Soak the sotanghon noodles until pliable. Sauté onion, garlic, and celery until fragrant. Add chicken and cook until lightly browned. Pour in broth and simmer until the chicken is cooked. Add carrots, cabbage, and the soaked noodles. Cook until the noodles are tender. Season with fish sauce, salt, and pepper. Garnish with green onions and fried garlic before serving.

Cook the rice noodles according to package directions and set aside. Prepare the sauce by sautéing garlic and adding ground pork or shrimp broth. Stir in annatto water and cornstarch slurry until thickened. Season with fish sauce and pepper. Arrange noodles on a platter, pour the sauce over them, and top with shrimp, smoked fish flakes, crushed pork cracklings, boiled eggs, green onions, and fried garlic. Serve with calamansi.

In a pot, combine coconut milk, evaporated milk, condensed milk, sugar, and corn kernels. Bring to a gentle simmer while stirring. In a bowl, dissolve cornstarch in water or milk, then slowly pour it into the coconut mixture while stirring constantly. Continue cooking until thick and smooth. Pour into a greased dish, level the top, and let it set. Sprinkle toasted coconut curds or latik on top before serving.

Cook glutinous rice with water until partially done. In a wide pan, combine coconut milk and brown sugar and cook until thickened. Add the cooked sticky rice and stir until the grains absorb the coconut caramel mixture. Continue cooking until the rice becomes glossy and sticky. Transfer to a greased pan and top with latik before serving.

Melt sugar in llaneras or a small pan until it turns into amber caramel, then let it set. In a bowl, gently mix egg yolks, evaporated milk, condensed milk, and vanilla without creating too many bubbles. Strain the custard mixture and pour it over the caramel. Cover the molds with foil and steam or bake in a water bath until set. Cool completely, then chill before unmolding.

In a bowl, combine grated cassava, coconut milk, condensed milk, evaporated milk, eggs, and sugar until well mixed. Pour into a greased baking pan and bake until mostly set. For the topping, mix coconut milk, condensed milk, egg yolks, and cheese if using. Pour the topping over the cassava layer and bake again until the top is golden and slightly firm. Cool before slicing.

In a bowl, combine flour, sugar, baking powder, milk, and egg until smooth. Stir in melted butter or oil. Pour the batter into small molds lined with paper cups. Top with cheese if desired. Steam over gently boiling water until the puto are fluffy and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean. Let cool slightly before removing from molds.

Prepare pandan-flavored gelatin according to package directions and let it set. Cut the gelatin into cubes. In a bowl, combine young coconut strips, nata de coco, kaong if using, cream, and condensed milk. Add the pandan gelatin cubes and mix gently. Chill well before serving.

Prepare tall glasses and add sweetened beans, kaong, nata de coco, macapuno, jackfruit, and other desired preserves. Fill the glass with shaved ice. Pour evaporated milk over the ice. Top with leche flan, ube halaya, and a scoop of ice cream if desired. Serve immediately and mix before eating.
